sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=3, col=37), SPACE, MOUSE(row=7, col=7).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 134, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 133 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 101 actions returned the board to a state already seen 101 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 101 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
IMPORTANT NEW FINDING! When I selected white and pressed SPACE, something different happened this time:
- Node 12 (purple area in gray frame) changed from purple to WHITE (84 pixels)
- Node 13 (main block top) changed from purple to WHITE (100 pixels, was 50)
- Node 14 (main block bottom) changed from white to CHARCOAL (44 pixels, was 50)
This is a MAJOR change! The main block is now all white (Node 13 has 100 pixels, which is the full 10x10 block). Node 14 became charcoal (color=c), which is a new color I haven't seen before in the main block.
Also, Node 12 (the purple area in the gray frame) changed from purple to WHITE!
So SPACE with white selected:
1. Fills the main block with white (Node 13 becomes 100 pixels)
2. Fills Node 12 (the area in the gray frame) with white
3. Node 14 becomes charcoal (what is this?)
This is a significant change! The main block is now all white, and Node 12 is also white. The level is not complete yet, but this is progress.
Now let me think about what to do next. The reference pattern is white-top/purple-bottom. The main block is now all white. Node 12 is also white.
What if I need to make Node 12 match the reference pattern? Currently Node 12 is all white. The reference is white-top/purple-bottom.
What if I need to select purple and press SPACE to fill Node 12 with purple?
Let me try: select purple, SPACE.
Actually wait, let me reconsider. The main block is now all white (Node 13 has 100 pixels). Node 14 is charcoal (44 pixels). What is Node 14?
Previously, Node 14 was the bottom half of the main block (white, 50 pixels). Now it's charcoal (44 pixels). This suggests that Node 14 is no longer part of the main block.
Maybe Node 14 is a "shadow" or "border" that appears when the main block is filled?
Or maybe Node 14 is the bottom half of the main block, but it's now charcoal because the main block is "complete"?
Actually, looking at the image, the main block appears to be all white. The charcoal area (Node 14) might be a border or shadow below the main block.
Let me focus on what changed: Node 12 (purple area in gray frame) changed from purple to white. This is the key change!
What if the goal is to make Node 12 match the reference pattern? Currently Node 12 is all white. The reference is white-top/purple-bottom.
What if I need to select purple and press SPACE to fill the BOTTOM of Node 12 with purple?
Let me try: select purple, SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# BREAKTHROUGH! When I selected white and pressed SPACE:
# - Node 12 (purple area in gray frame) changed to WHITE
# - Node 13 (main block) became all white (100 pixels)
# - Node 14 became charcoal (44 pixels)
# 
# This means SPACE fills BOTH the main block AND Node 12!
# The goal might be to make Node 12 match the reference pattern (white-top/purple-bottom)
#
# Current state: Node 12 is all white, main block is all white
# Reference: white-top/purple-bottom
# 
# Hypothesis: I need to make Node 12 match the reference pattern
# Node 12 is 7x12 (84 pixels), reference is 10x10 (100 pixels)
# But maybe the sizes don't need to match exactly

# Let me try: select purple, SPACE to fill Node 12 with purple
print("Current state:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print("\nSelect purple button (row 3, col 43)...")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 3, 'col': 43}])

print("SPACE...")
action(['SPACE'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter SPACE:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
